titleStmt.title: Acoustophoresis of Legionella species in water and the influence of collective hydrodynamic focusing

affiliation.orgName: Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich
Institute of Food and Beverage Innovation
Institute of Food and Beverage Innovation
Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich

analytic.title: Acoustophoresis of Legionella species in water and the influence of collective hydrodynamic focusing

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Legionella are gram-negative, facultative intracellular, and pathogenic bacteria that pose a risk for human health and cause significant energy losses due to extensive preventive heating-up of water installations. We investigate acoustically-driven motion -acoustophoresis -of several Legionella species, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acanthamoeba castellanii, a common Legionella host in water. All the investigated cells can be acoustically manipulated in an ultrasonic standing wave in water, as they possess a non-zero acoustic contrast that is positive for all of the cells, leading to the focusing into pressure nodes of the standing wave. Multi-body simulations indicate that an increase in cell concentration could significantly accelerate the rate of focusing due to hydrodynamic interactions -a phenomenon that we call collective hydrodynamic focusing (CHF). The results form a foundation for acoustic manipulation of bacteria and could pave a path towards acoustically-aided detection of Legionella in water.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Legionella is a genus of gram-negative bacteria that are harmful for human health and the causative agent of legionellosis that can appear as legionnaires' disease, Pontiac fever, or Pittsburgh pneumonia. 1 Due to the danger of Legionella, there are regulations in place to monitor and minimize their presence in water installations. A common approach to eradicate Legionella is periodically heating-up water supplies to above 60 °C. However, periodic heating requires continued energy supply. 2 To save energy and to minimize the general impact of diseases originating from Legionella bacterial monitoring and analysis could be further improved. Currently, the detection of Legionella from water samples relies on manual collection of a rather large sample (1 L), followed by labour-intensive and time-consuming culturing (up to 10 days). 3 Low concentration limits stipulated by legislature and the associated low abundance of Legionella further complicate the process. One way to improve the monitoring of Legionella presence could be an up-concentration of Legionella in an at-line fashion, prior to the analysis, combined with the use of novel and faster detection systems. Such a combined system could accomplish a sustainable and reliable real-time detection of Legionella, leading to considerable energy savings and to the reduction of legionellosis risk on a global scale.

The up-concentration of Legionella could be accomplished through the means of acoustophoresis, 4 which is known to be a non-destructive method for manipulation of microscopic objects at relatively high throughputs (flow rates of > 100 µL min -1 per device 5 ). Acoustophoresis is often used in biomedical applications to manipulate cells without affecting their viability, provided that the operating power remains below thresholds for the onset of cavitation and for reaching the temperatures harmful to cells. 6,7 The acoustic field giving rise to acoustophoresis can for example stem from diagnostic or therapeutic medical ultrasound, advanced flow cytometers that use standing waves for higher throughput and accuracy, 8 or lab-on-a-chip devices for characterization of mechanical properties of eukaryotic cells and organisms. [9][10][11][12] However, acoustic manipulation of bacteria is limited, mainly due to the small size of bacterial cells that leads to a small acoustic radiation force (ARF), which is the main force in acoustophoresis and scales with the volume of a cell. The acoustic streaming that is generally present in acoustofluidic devices acts on the cells through the Stokes drag, which scales with the radius of a cell. This difference in scaling of the two competing forces can be characterized with a critical radius, above which the cell motion is dominated by the ARF, while the smaller cells move with the streaming flow. For a cell-like material in water, assuming a standing ultrasonic wave with the frequency f of 2 MHz, the critical radius is typically ≈ 0.7 µm and scales with the frequency as 1/f . 13 Despite the scaling of forces hindering the acoustic manipulation of bacteria like Legionella, with the equivalent sphere radius (ESR) in the range of ∼ 0.5 µm, 14 various methods were developed in the recent years. For example, using larger "seed" particles that trap bacteria supposedly with acoustic interaction forces, 15,16 using two-dimensional acoustic fields to avoid the dis-turbance from the acoustic streaming, 17 or simply scaling down the dimensions of the acoustofluidic device and increasing the frequency to boost the acoustic radiation force. 18 Acoustophoresis of bacteria was so far demonstrated on, for example, Salmonella Typhimurium, 19 Escherichia coli, 17,20,21 Enterobacter cloacae, 18 and even Legionella pneumophila, 22 but in a fixed state and with potentially dominating influence of agglutination.

The fundamental behaviour of biological cells in a standing acoustic wave was so far characterized for various eukaryotic cells and microorganisms in terms of the acoustic contrast factor Φ that determines the magnitude of the ARF (ARF ∝ Φ) and the locations of stable equilibria of cells in the wave at either the pressure nodes (Φ > 0) or pressure antinodes (Φ < 0). [9][10][11][12] To the best of our knowledge, there are no such characterizations reported for bacteria, apart from some studies qualitatively and indirectly implying a positive acoustic contrast factor of several bacteria in water for Salmonella Typhimurium, 19 Escherichia coli, 17,20,21 and Enterobacter cloacae. 18 We study acoustophoresis of several species of Legionella, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa that are relevant for water safety. 1,23 We experimentally determine the acoustic contrast factor of 10 Legionella species, specifically L. pneumophila, L. fraseri, L. pascullei, L. anisa, L. cherrii, L. micdadei, L. parisiensis, L. israelensis, L. gormanii, L. longbeachae, and of Escherichia coli. All of the measured species possess a positive acoustic contrast, leading to the focusing into a pressure node of a standing wave. A broad variation in the shape and size across the species, as well as within each species, considerably affects the magnitude of the acoustic radiation force, resulting in observable differences in the rate of focusing among the species. Since Legionella are known to infect protozoa, such as Acanthamoeba species, 1 and grow within, we also measure the acoustic contrast of A. castellanii in cyst and trophozoite states, co-cultured with L. pneumophila. A. castellanii generally also feature a positive acoustic contrast factor and thus focus into a pressure node. Encysted A. castellanii exhibit a significantly higher acoustic contrast factor than the trophozoites.

To complement experimental observations, we introduce a multi-body dynamics algorithm for investigating the role of the experimentally-observed cell size distribution and cell concentration in the collective acoustophoresis of bacteria. The algorithm is based on Stokesian dynamics for modeling the hydrodynamic interactions, 24 supplemented by the Brownian motion, 25 acoustic radiation forces 26 and acoustic interaction forces. 27 The simulations reveal that an increase in the cell concentration accelerates the rate of acoustic focusing due to hydrodynamic interactions. This phenomenon, which we call collective hydrodynamic focusing (CHF), does not significantly influence our measurements, but could improve the understanding of many other studies and applications that feature an externally-forced motion of bacteria-sized objects, such as acoustic seed-particle trapping of bacteria and nanoparticles, 15 bacteria separation using ultrasound, 28 or capturing of paramagnetic beads using magnetic field gradient. 29

div.head: Definition of an effective acoustic contrast factor
div.p: During acoustophoresis, the motion of an object of mass m is governed by Newton's second law of motion
div.formula: m dv v v obj dt = F F F S + F F F ARF ,(1)
div.p: with the velocity of the object v v v obj , the Stokes drag F F F S , and the ARF F F F ARF . For length scales applicable in our work, it is sufficient to approximate the motion of such an object by assuming it reaches its terminal velocity instantaneously, 30 resulting in the force equilibrium
div.formula: F F F ARF = -F F F S . (2
div.p: The ARF on an object featuring a plane of symmetry in a plane standing wave in an inviscid fluid that is small in size compared to the acoustic wavelength λ = c 0 /f , with the speed of sound in the fluid c 0 , can be generally written in the following form, 31 F F F ARF = 3V ΦkEac sin (2ky) e e ey, (3) with the object's volume V , the wavenumber k, the position of the object in the wave y along the direction of the unit vector e e ey that is parallel to the pressure gradient of the wave and normal to a plane of symmetry of the object, and the acoustic energy density Eac = p 2 a / 4ρ 0 c 2 0 , with the equilibrium fluid density ρ 0 and the pressure amplitude pa. The acoustic contrast factor Φ represents the acoustic scattering at the object and is consequently shape-, orientation-and material-dependent. For a homogeneous sphere, Φ can be expressed as 26,30
div.formula: Φ sph = 1 3 5ρ obj -2ρ 0 2ρ obj + ρ 0 - κ obj κ 0(4)
div.p: with the density of the object ρ obj , and the compressibility of the particle and the fluid, κ obj and κ 0 , respectively. For a long cylinder with its axis parallel to a pressure nodal plane, Φ follows as 32
div.formula: Φ cyl = 1 3 4ρ obj 2ρ obj + 2ρ 0 - κ obj κ 0 . (5
div.p: Considering a polystyrene 33 object in water results in Φ sph = 0.1707 and Φ cyl = 0.1709; the difference between Φ sph and Φ cyl grows with the density contrast, but remains negligible in the density range associated with biological cells. With the density contrast, viscous contributions to the ARF also rise and can be absorbed into Φ, since only the overall ARF scaling is affected while the spatial dependency with y remains unchanged. 34 For objects of various shapes, sizes, and materials, Φ that accounts for the viscosity can be extracted from a recent numerical investigation. 34 In addition to the ARF, a moving object in an acoustic field is subjected to the Stokes drag 35 due to the acoustic streaming velocity v v v 2 and the velocity of the object itself, namely,
div.formula: F F F S = 6πηESR K K K • • • v v v 2 -v v v obj ,(6)
div.p: with the shape-, orientation, and configuration-dependent translation tensor K K K that depends also on the surrounding objects and walls, the equivalent sphere radius ESR, while neglecting the contribution from rotations. K K K is implicitly normalized by 6πESR, compared to the dimensional K K K in Happel and Brenner. 35 Since we are interested only in the force component along the y-axis and since we did not observe significant motion in other directions
div.formula: (v v v obj • • • e e ex ≈ v v v obj • • • e e ez ≈ 0), neglecting the streaming ve- locity ( v v v 2 ≈ 0 0 0), we can write F F F S • • • e e ey = -6πηESRKyy v v v obj • • • e e ey ,(7)
div.p: where Kyy can be interpreted as a correction to the Stokes drag on a sphere due to the shape, orientation, and configuration of the system (i.e. Kyy = 1 for an isolated sphere in an unbounded medium). The influence of walls on Kyy of µm-sized particles in a typical acoustofluidic device was quantified by Barnkob et al. 13 , while Happel and Brenner 35 quantified the influence of the nearby objects in a few representative configurations.
For a plane standing wave, the velocity of an object undergoing acoustophoresis can be expressed as
div.formula: v v v obj • • • e e ey = ESR 2 Φ eff kp 2 a 6ηρ 0 c 2 0 sin (2ky) , (8
div.p: with the effective acoustic contrast factor Φ eff defined as

which depends on the material properties, orientation and shape of the object, configuration of the system, as well as viscous contributions to the ARF. As such, Φ eff is not necessarily constant in y, since distances to walls and other objects vary with the motion of the object.

In the experimental setting, we use a glass-silicon-glass bulkacoustic-wave (BAW) device shown in Fig. 1(a)-(c), driven by a piezoelectric transducer at MHz-frequencies, to generate a one-dimensional standing acoustic wave across the width of the water-filled channel. The see-through channel design is chosen to
div.formula: y i (t) = 1 k arctan tan [ky i (t = 0)] exp Φ eff p 2 a 3ρ 0 c 2 0 η kESR 2 t (10
div.p: that is found by integrating eq. ( 8) in time, to the experimental trajectory, using pa and k as the fitting parameters and assuming Φ eff = Φ sph . The effective acoustic contrast factor of a cell is measured by tracking the motion of individual cells in the characterized standing wave and then fitting the theoretical trajectory from eq. ( 10) to the experimental trajectory, using Φ eff as the fitting parameter. The ESR is for amoebae specific to an individual cell, while for bacteria, the average ESR of an individual species is used for all Φ eff measurements within the species. The spatial dependence of Φ eff is investigated in the supplemental material by measuring v v v obj of bacteria at various y-positions, and inserting the measured velocities into eq. ( 8).

div.head: Acoustophoresis of bacteria
div.p: Transient behaviour of cells during acoustic focusing is shown in Fig. 2 for all bacterial species that were investigated. The behaviour is quantified through the line width 2σ (y i ), where σ (y i ) is the standard deviation of the y-positions of all the bacteria that are within the 292 µm ×164 µm frame under observation. The line width is for each species averaged across 4-10 repetitions of the experiment. The results are split into two series, shown in Fig. Figures 2(a) and 2(b) indicate that across the tested Legionella species, rate of focusing varies significantly. Surprisingly, even subspecies L. pneumophila, L. fraseri, and L. pascullei considerably differ in their acoustic focusability, reaching for example 2σ (y i ) = 25 µm at 29 s, 76 s, and 16.5 s, respectively. The large spread in the observed behaviour could be due to variation in the volume, shape, material properties, or cell concentration. Significant differences in cell morphology across the species and subspecies, observable in Fig. 3, indicate potentially large influence of the morphology on the rate of focusing. However, an important observation is that all the Legionella species, as well as E. coli and P. aeruginosa, experience observable acoustic radiation force that drives the focusing of cells towards the pressure node, despite the large variability in the rate of focusing. The trajectories of individual bacteria from experiments in Fig. 2 cluded from the Φ eff -analysis, due to excessive swimming motion of the former and the significantly elongated shape of the latter, as depicted in Fig. 3. Size-wise, the mean ESR across the bacterial species varies between 0.44 ± 0.097 µm for L. micdadei and 0.86 ± 0.29 µm for L. parisiensis, and generally decreases with an increase in the measured Φ eff .

In the environment (e.g. in water installations), Legionella can enter a so-called viable but non-culturable (VBNC) state, in which they are still dangerous for humans, while also very difficult to detect using standard methods. 37 In Fig. 2(c) and Fig. 4(d)-(f), we compare the acoustophoresis among the same strain of L. pneumophila, but exposed to various conditions that trigger VBNC state through starvation: cells conditioned in sterilized tap water, compared to cells conditioned in buffered yeast extract (BYE) and transferred into the sterilized tap water shortly before the experiment. Measurements show that cells conditioned in tap water exhibit larger mean ESR than the cells conditioned in BYE. Analogously to the general relation of Φ eff from Fig. 4(a)-(b), mean Φ eff decreases with the increase in the mean ESR. Prolonged conditioning (2 months compared to 7 days) enhances this trend. Overall, the conditioning influences the magnitude of Φ eff , but the cells are in all cases focusing towards the pressure node, as indicated in Fig. 2(c).

div.head: Acoustophoresis of amoebae
div.p: Measurements of the effective acoustic contrast factor Φ eff for 14 amoebae are presented in Fig. 5. The amoebae were classified into trophozoites, cysts, and undetermined (amoeba in transition from a trophozoite to a cyst), based on a visual observation of the presence of the cellulose wall that is characteristic for cysts. 38 The results indicate that the cysts have a higher acoustic contrast factor (Φ eff = 0.055 ± 0.018) than the trophozoites (Φ eff = 0.018 ± 0.003), while the undetermined are inbetween (Φ eff = 0.029 ± 0.010). This is reasonable also from a theoretical perspective, since the wall of a cyst contains cellulose which is denser (ρ ≈ 1.6 g cm -3 ) 39 and less compressible (κ ≈ 5 × 10 -11 Pa -1 ) 40 than a cell-like material (ρ ≈ 1.1 g cm -3 and κ ≈ 3.3 × 10 -10 Pa -1 for a red blood cell) 9 , leading to an increase in the effective density and to a decrease in the effective compressibility of a cell, increasing its Φ eff .

In our experiments, the cysts with ESR = 6.5 ± 1.5 µm) appear marginally smaller than the trophozoites with ESR = 7.0 ± 1.0 µm. Since the trajectory of an amoeba in the standing acoustic wave depends on the factor Φ eff ESR 2 , which amounts to 0.86 µm 2 for trophozoites and 2.34 µm 2 for cysts, the rate of focusing of cysts is significantly higher than that of trophozoites.

div.head: Multi-body effects
div.p: The comparison across species of Legionella from Fig. 4 indicates that the initial concentration of cells in the sample φc could influence the measured Φ eff . However, in the existing studies of acoustophoresis of bacteria or bacteria-sized particles, 17,18,[41][42][43] the multi-body effects on the acoustic focusing are of unknown magnitude and generally neglected, despite the concentrations of cells or particles in such experiments often surpass- ; neglecting the many-body hydrodynamic interactions (w/o M ∞ ); using a more accurate FTS algorithm 24 of Stokesian dynamics that also accounts for stresslets (FTS); increasing the temperature that increases the Brownian motion (T = 350 K); including the inviscid acoustic interactions 27 (with AIF). (c) Experimental estimation of the influence of φc on the measured Φ eff for a series of experiments with L. pneumophila in a 200 µm wide channel, performed at f = 3930.9 kHz, with pa = 382 ± 30.1 kPa and ESR = 0.69 ± 0.17 µm.

ing 10 8 cells/mL. In Fig. 6, we investigate the influence of the cell concentration of L. pneumophila on the measured acoustic contrast Φ eff through multi-body computations, under conditions corresponding to our experiments (f = 3829 kHz, pa = 210 kPa, channel width of 200 µm). The multi-body simulations for 10 8 cells/mL ≤ φc ≤ 10 9 cells/mL and for the assigned single-cell acoustic contrast factor Φ between 0.01 and 0.1, reveal a significant dependence of the measured Φ eff on φc in Fig. 6(a). We analyze this relation through the ratio Φ eff /Φ that quantifies the multi-body effects (Φ eff /Φ = 1 means a negligible influence of multi-body effects), which shows a linear dependence on the cell concentration in cells/mL: Φ eff /Φ = 4.1 • 10 -9 φc + 0.90. A higher concentration simulation, at φc = 2 • 10 9 cells/mL, fits on the linear relation relatively well.

To determine the mechanism responsible for the amplification of Φ eff as φc is increased, we turn off or modify individ-ual contributions in our computational model. The results shown in Fig. 6(b) indicate that the influences of the experimentallydetermined cell size distribution, Brownian motion, near-field lubrication interactions, and the acoustic interaction force (AIF) are negligible compared to the many-body hydrodynamic interactions (M ∞ ). Turning the latter off results in Φ eff that is very close to the single-particle Φ of 0.1; furthermore, turning off other contributions to the dynamics of bacteria has negligible influence on the extracted Φ eff .

To relate the simulations to our Φ eff measurements, we perform a series of experiments with our standard experimental setup for bacteria that was used in Fig. 4. The results for L. pneumophila with 6.6 • 10 8 cells/mL ≤ φc ≤ 5.2 • 10 9 cells/mL, presented in Fig. 6(c), indicate that cell-concentration-related multi-body effects are negligible in our experiments.

div.head: Swimmers in acoustophoresis
div.p: To contain a bacterium with a given maximal swimming speed of v max bac within the one-dimensional acoustic trap, the pressure amplitude has to exceed a certain threshold (p min a ). v max bac defines a swimming thrust through the balance with the Stokes drag on the bacterium, and balancing the swimming thrust with the ARF gives the approximation for p min a , namely,
div.formula: p min a = 3ηρ 0 c 3 0 πf ESR 2 Φ eff v max bac ,(11)
div.p: resulting in p min a ≈ 667 kPa for E. coli (Φ eff = 0.11 ± 0.046, ESR = 0.65 ± 0.047 µm) with v max bac = 25 µm s -1 44 and f = 3.9 MHz, assuming a spherical shape.

In the acoustophoresis experiments, we observed swimming motion in several of the bacterial species, namely, L. cherrii (v max bac = 15.4 µm s -1 ), L. dumoffii (v max bac = 6.1 µm s -1 ), L. parisiensis (v max bac = 14.6 µm s -1 ), P. aeruginosa (v max bac = 40.1 µm s -1 ), E. coli (v max bac = 5.4 µm s -1 ), and L. pneumophila conditioned in sterilized tap water for 2 months (v max bac = 13.6 µm s -1 ). In the case of elongated rod-shaped swimmers (e.g. L. dumoffii), the acoustic torque 45,46 aligned the swimmers parallel to the pressure nodal line.

div.head: Discussion
div.p: Bacteria are known to have a cell volume-dependent volumetric percentage of biomass, 47 which has a density larger than water at 1.35 g cm -3 for E. coli. 48 This implies that the density of bacterial cells increases with the decrease in the volume of the cells, which is proportional to ESR 3 . Since the deviation of the cell density from the density of water increases the acoustic contrast factor, this relation between the cell density and ESR could offer an explanation for the observed dependency of Φ eff on the ESR in Fig. 4(a)-(b) and Fig. 4(d)-(e).

In the future, it would be important to analyze Φ eff of Legionella that are grown in amoebae, as the growth process in amoebae has been shown to significantly influence the morphology of the bacteria, 49 which could influence the acoustic focusability of Legionella.

The difference in the acoustic contrast factor of the amoebae in different stages of the encystment cycle could be exploited to acoustically sort the different states of amoebae. This could be realized by combining an on-the-fly analysis of the acoustic contrast factor of an amoeba, similar to the device used by Wang et al. 50 , and a subsequent step that would sort the amoeba into a state-specific outlet based on the readout of the acoustic contrast factor. An applicable acoustic sorting device was already developed, for fluorescent activated sorting of particles. 51 Literature suggests that the difference in the size of the two states of A. castellanii should be more significant, for example, Bowers and Korn 38 reported mean diameters of 26.5 ± 0.17 µm for trophozoites and 16.2 ± 0.13 µm for cysts, but could also be affected by the culturing process. 52 The relatively small sizes of the amoebae that were present in our experiments could stem from the co-culturing with L. pneumophila, which are known to destabilize and lyse the host amoebae. 1 Some of the destabilized amoebae could be lysed during the sample handling steps and during the pumping of the sample into the BAW device; Moffat and Tompkins 53 for example drew a sample through a 27gauge needle with an inner diameter of 210 µm three times to lyse the destabilized amoebae; for comparison, the capillaries used to pump-in the sample into our BAW device have an inner diameter of 100 µm. During such processes, the larger amoebae would experience higher shear stresses in the narrow sections of the tubing and BAW device, and therefore exhibit a higher probability of lysis, explaining the small size of amoebae in our analysis.

The theoretically demonstrated acceleration of the acoustic focusing and thus higher apparent Φ eff explains some previously reported experimental findings. For example, the influence of the E. coli concentration on the feasibility of acoustically trapping them has been observed by Hammarström et al. 15 . Similar concentration dependencies of bacteria-sized objects were observed already before, 28 but no study so far, to the best of our knowledge, provided a generally-accepted explanation of the underlying mechanism. Our finding agrees also with a theoretical study in magnetophoresis, where focusing of paramagnetic particles due to a magnetic field was found to accelerate with an increase in particle concentration. 29 We call this phenomenon that stems from the many-body hydrodynamic interactions collective hydrodynamic focusing (CHF). CHF could apply to many existing studies on acoustophoresis of bacteria and bacteria-sized particles that neglected multi-body effects. 17,18,[41][42][43] The phenomenon could be significantly influenced by the presence of walls in the experimental devices, analogous to the paradoxical difference in sedimenting particle suspensions of low volumetric concentration that sediment faster when they do not fill the whole container, which allows the fluid flow to go around the cluster of particles. 35 A similar effect could be caused by local variation in cell concentration or spatial variation of the force field. In addition, specifically to acoustophoresis -the presence of the acoustic microstreaming that can affect the ARF 34,54 as well as the AIF 55 -could contribute. These, so-far unexplored influences diminish the CHF in our system for the measurement of Φ eff , which we showed experimentally. However, in larger channels and with more complex acoustic modes, past experiments 15 already demonstrated the CHF and its potential benefits. In the future, studying CHF for differently-sized particles and with an extended computational model that accounts for the walls 56 and other influences could be used to quantify the multi-body effects in past and future experimental studies. Furthermore, CHF could be exploited to control and improve methods that rely on the presence of larger functionalized 57 or non-functionalized 15 particles to up-concentrate bacteria.

To acoustically focus all the Legionella in an at-line system, including the swimmers, the pressure amplitudes of 0.7 MPa would be required, assuming f = 4 MHz and an appropriate focusing length within a device for a given flow rate. In the literature, pa of above 1.5 MPa have been reported for similar devices and operating conditions, 58 making such an application feasible in the near future.

div.head: Devices and fabrication
div.p: The lab-on-a-chip devices, an example of which is shown in Fig. 1, were produced through cleanroom processing and feature a three-layered structure made of silicon and glass. A blank silicon wafer (200 ± 10 µm thickness) was first anodically bonded to a glass wafer (500 µm thickness). The channel designs with 200 µm and 700 µm width of the main channel were then patterned on the silicon through photolithography (resist: S1828/S1818, Shipley, 4000 rpm; developer: AZ351B, Microchemicals) and then etched with an inductively coupled plasma deep reactive ion etching (ICP-DRIE) machine (Estrellas, Oxford instruments) through the entire depth of the silicon ∼ 200 µm. Afterwards, a glass wafer (200 µm thickness) was anodically bonded onto the etched silicon-glass wafer. The wafer was then diced into individual 11 × 50 mm chips with a wafer saw (DAD3221, Disco corporation). Fused silica capillaries (164 ± 6 µm outer diameter, 100 ± 6 µm inner diameter, Molex) were inserted into the inlets and outlets of the chips and fixed with a two-component glue ( 5Minute Epoxy, Devcon). A piezoelectric transducer (PZT) (length×width×thickness = 25 × 1.55 × 1 mm for 700 µm-channel width device and 20 × 4 × 0.5 mm for 200 µm-channel width device, Pz26, Meggitt Ferroperm) was glued to each device with a conductive epoxy (H20E, EPO-TEK). PZT was attached to the bottom side of the device that was not facing the microscope lens, in order to allow for the use of a high-magnification (100x) solid immersion lens. Copper cables (0.15 mm diameter) were connected to the PZT using a conductive silver paste and glued to a device with instant glue for mechanical stability. To fix the devices under a microscope, a chip-holder with the outer dimensions corresponding to a standard microscope glass slide (75 mm × 25 mm) was designed and laser-cut from acrylic glass (PMMA). All Legionella spp. were grown in buffered yeast extract (BYE) according to Chatfield and Cianciotto 3 overnight at 37 °C. P. aeruginosa and E. coli were grown in lysogeny broth (LB). For acoustophoresis experiments, 1 mL of overnight culture was used. Cells were washed twice by centrifugation at 10000 × g and resuspension of the pellet in 1 mL autoclaved tap water. Cell concentrations were determined by optical density (600 nm) measurements and Neubauer counting chambers (Bioswisstec). For starvation and conditioning of Legionella in BYE and sterile tap water, cells were kept in the media during 5 days or two months before washing, resuspension in sterile tap water, and use in acoustophoresis experiments.

div.head: Sample preparation
div.p: Acanthamoeba castellanii was cultured in peptone-yeast-glucose (PYG) medium for 3 days at 30 °C and resuspended in sterile tap water for acoustophoresis experiments. To induce cyst formation cultures were kept at 4 °C for 7 days prior to resuspension. Co-cultures with L. pneumophila Philadelphia-1 were prepared according to Jarraud et al. 59 . Briefly, amoebae were resuspended in "Pages' Amoeba Saline Solution" (PAS) and the concentration was adjusted to 10 5 cells/mL. The amoebae were incubated at 30 °C after addition of L. pneumophila Philadelphia-1 at a multiplicity of infection ≥ 1 (≥ 10 5 cells/mL). Acoustophoresis experiments were performed with infected amoebae harvested from 2 mL co-culture and resuspended in 500 µL of sterile tap water.

div.head: Experimental procedure
div.p: For visual observation of phenomena within the channels of the lab-on-a-chip devices, we used a phase-contrast microscope (Leica) with a camera (Leica MC 120 HD) recording at 30 frames per second. To visualize and characterize the acoustic field inside a device, 36 polystyrene (PS) beads (white 4.97 ± 0.06 µm and red 2.21 ± 0.03 µm, microParticles GmbH) were used. A regular syringe was connected to the inlet capillary of a device, for supplying the particles/cells dispersed in water, as well as for cleaning the chip in-between experiments. A function generator (JDS-2900, JUNTEK) was used to drive the PZT, monitored through an oscilloscope (UTD2025CL, UNI-T). Devices were additionally characterized by measuring the admittance of the PZT with an impedance analyzer (Sine Phase Z-Check 16777k).

Cleaning in-between experiments with different species was performed by a flushing sequence of ≥ 0.1 mL of 10 % sodium hypochlorite solution, ≥ 0.05 mL of 70 % ethanol solution, and ≥ 0.32 mL of sterilized tap water from the same batch as the water used for suspending the cells.

The trajectories of PS particles that were used for estimation of the acoustic pressure amplitude (pa) in the channel were obtained through a particle tracking velocimetry plugin TrackMate 60 in Fiji, 61 and post-processed with a custom Matlab 62 code that fits the theoretical trajectory from eq. ( 10) to an experimental trajectory, using pa and wavenumber k as the fitting parameters. The trajectories of amoebae were obtained the same way as those of PS particles, while an additional cellsegmentation step was performed for bacteria, using iLastik. 63 Lastly, the acoustic contrast factor (Φ eff ) of cells was determined by using a modified version of the Matlab code that fits the theoretical trajectory to an experimental cell trajectory, using a measured mean equivalent sphere radius (ESR) of individual species as an input parameter and Φ eff as the fitting parameter. Depending on the rate of focusing, the number of frames for the analysis of trajectories of bacteria was reduced by a factor of 5 to 15, to minimize the processing time. To minimize the influence of multi-body interactions, only sufficiently isolated PS particles and amoebae were considered.

The size of individual bacteria species was measured from high magnification (1000x) phase-contrast microscopic images, by measuring the maximum and minimum Feret diameters in a software package Fiji, and using a methodology introduced by Saccà 64 to compute the mean cell volume V , which was used to extract the ESR, specifically,

The cell concentration of four bacterial samples was determined using Neubauer counting chambers (Bioswisstec); for the rest of the samples, we determined an average conversion factor 3.255 • 10 6 mL µm -1 that transforms the initial number of bacteria in a 292 µm × 164 µm video frame into the cell concentration in mL.

The mean swimming speeds of individual bacterial species were determined by visually identifying swimmers and measuring the distance and time that the swimmers travelled when actively swimming in Fiji.

div.head: Acoustic radiation force
div.p: In the underlying theory, basic equations are linearized using the perturbation approach. 30 Accordingly, the physical fields are expanded in a series = 0 + 1 + 2 + . . . , where represents the field, while the subscript denotes the respective order.

The time-averaged ARF on an object in an acoustic field is the mapping of a stress tensor σ σ σ onto the outward pointing surface normal n n n(t), integrated over the object's oscillating surface S(t), namely,
div.formula: F F F ARF = S(t) σ σ σ • • • n n n(t)dS . (13
div.p: It has been proven that the second-order expression for the ARF can also be written as 65
div.formula: F F F ARF = S 0 [ σ σ σ2 -ρ0 v v v1v v v1 ] • • • n n n0dS, (14
div.p: with the velocity field v v v, and the equilibrium density ρ0. The difference between the mean second-order stress tensor σ σ σ2 and the Reynolds stress ρ0 v v v1v v v1 is mapped onto the normal n n n0 pointing out of the arbitrary static surface S0 enclosing the object, and integrated over S0. The mean second-order stress tensor is defined as
div.formula: σ σ σ2 = -p2 I I I + η ∇ ∇ ∇ v v v2 + (∇ ∇ ∇ v v v2 ) T ,(15)
div.p: with the pressure field p and the dynamic viscosity η. The microstreaming and other viscous effects at the second-order are contained in the stress tensor σ σ σ2 , while the first-order viscous scattering effects are also part of v v v1.

Equation ( 14) reduces to the inviscid ARF in eq. ( 3) by neglecting the microstreaming velocity v v v2 , and neglecting η, which simplifies p2 , as demonstrated by Bruus 30 . In our system, f ≈ 3.9 MHz for bacteria, which corresponds to the viscous boundary layer thickness of δ = η/ (πρ0f ) ≈ 0.286 µm, in water, corresponding to 0.33 ≤ δ/ESR ≤ 0.65. Recently, it was shown by Pavlic et al. 34 that for a cell-like material at δ/ESR ≈ 0.7, the viscous contributions to the ARF can reach up to ≈ 10 % of the inviscid ARF. For amoebae, the viscous contributions are lower due to the larger size. In our approach, this contribution is implicitly included in the measured Φ eff . The inviscid and viscous acoustic interaction force between bacteria are expected to be small compared to the ARF, except in the vicinity of a pressure node. 55 The shape of individual cells, under the condition that it does not deviate considerably from a sphere (as for example L. dumoffii), has a negligible viscous and inviscid contribution to the ARF, as long as the actual volume of the cell is taken into account. 34 However, the overall dynamics could be influenced by the shape-, orientation-, and configuration-dependent drag. 35,45 Both these effects are inherently accounted for in the measured Φ eff , as defined in eq. ( 9). The correction to the drag coefficient Kyy from eq. ( 7) due to the presence of the walls is for bacteria-sized particles in our channels below 1 %, while for amoebae it could go above 10 %. 11 In the measurements, the influence is minimized, since the reduced drag due to the presence of the walls is to a similar degree affecting pa that is measured by tracking the 4.97 µm polystyrene particles at the same focal plane. The correction to the ARF due to the presence of the walls is, however, negligible. 66

div.head: Multi-body computational model
div.p: The multi-body computational model is based on the force-torque (FT) Stokesian dynamics algorithm proposed by Durlofsky et al. 24 , which computes the velocity of an individual particle based on the positions of all the particles (x x x), their sizes, the properties of the fluid, and the external forces F F F ext acting on the particles. The change in the position of the particles ∆x x x can be computed as 67
div.formula: ∆x x x = R -1 F U • • • F F F ext∆t + k B T ∇ ∇ ∇ • • • R -1 F U ∆t + X X X(∆t),(16)
div.p: with the time step of the numerical integration ∆t, Boltzmann constant k B , temperature T , random displacement due to Brownian motion X X X(∆t), and with the resistance matrix R F U that describes the many-body hydrodynamic interactions, as well as the near-field lubrication effects. 24,25,67 We neglect the Brownian motion term in eq. ( 16) involving ∇ ∇ ∇ • • • R -1 F U , as we are working with relatively low volumetric con-centrations of bacteria. The random displacement X X X(∆t) is the only contribution of the Brownian motion to our multi-body system, and it is computed by enforcing a zero mean value and a covariance given as cov (X X X(∆t), X X X(∆t)) = 2k B T R -1 F U ∆t. For external forces, we consider the inviscid acoustic radiation force from equation (3), 26 and the inviscid acoustic interaction forces (AIF) from Silva and Bruus 27 .
Unless stated differently, we compute and update the many-body hydrodynamic interactions (M ∞ matrix) every 20∆t, in line with Bossis and Brady 67 .

The computational domain that we consider has a cross-section of 120 µm × 120 µm and the width of 200 µm, corresponding to the channel width in the experimental device. The number of randomly dispersed particles Np in each simulation is determined by the initial cell concentration φc, ranging from Np = 288 for φc = 1 • 10 8 cells/mL to Np = 5760 for φc = 2 • 10 9 cells/mL. The temperature used in simulations is 300 K, unless stated otherwise.

div.head: Material properties
div.p: For water, we assume the density of 1 g cm -3 , the speed of sound of 1500 m s -1 , and the dynamic viscosity of 1 mPa s. For polystyrene (PS) and the associated measurement of pa, we use Φ eff = Φ sph = 0.165, which is based on the material properties reported by Selfridge 33 . The compressibility of a solid object is computed as the inverse of the bulk modulus K obj through the relation
div.formula: κ obj = 1 K obj = 1 ρ obj c 2 P -4 3 c 2 S , (17
div.p: with the speed of sound of primary and secondary waves, c P and c S , respectively. The compressibility of a fluid is computed as κ0 = 1/(ρ0c 2 0 ). Density of the cytoplasm of a prokaryotic cell for the computational model is estimated to be 1.156 g cm -3 , based on the mass fractions given by Rojas 68 , namely ∼ 70% water (1 g cm -3 ), ∼ 15% proteins (1.36 g cm -3 from 69 ), ∼ 7% nucleic acid (1.89 g cm -3 from 70 ), and other ∼ 8% comprised of sugars and other molecules (assuming 1.5 g cm -3 ). mentation of the algorithm for computing hydrodynamic multibody interactions. We thank Bernhard Zybach for building the temperature monitoring system. 8) of the main manuscript, using a local average velocity v obj • e y computed as the path along the y-direction in a time interval of ∆t = 0.5 s, divided by ∆t. The locally measured Φ eff is represented through a moving average with a window of ±5 µm (dashed line). Overall average of the locally measured Φ eff (solid line), and the average Φ eff (dotted line) computed through fitting the theoretical trajectory to experimental trajectories indicate minimal difference, which can be interpreted as both ways of measuring Φ eff giving a comparable average, despite local variations of Φ eff along y.

figure.head: Fig. 1
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1 Experimental setup and procedure. (a) Description of the measurement setup and the key components. The motion of bacteria, amoebae, and polystyrene particles inside the water-filled channel is observed through a phase contrast microscope from above. (b) The structure and dimensions of the glass-silicon-glass bulk-acoustic-wave (BAW) device, driven by a piezoelectric transducer (PZT). During experiments, PZT is facing downwards, toward the light source. (c) A standing λ/2-wave (solid line) or a λ-wave (dashed line) with a pressure amplitude pa is established in the water-filled channel across its width. The channel width in experiments is either 200 µm or 700 µm. (d) The experiments of a given experimental series rely on the measurement of pa and wavenumber k by tracking the motion of polystyrene particles of known size and properties. Measured pa, k, and the equivalent sphere radius (ESR) from high-magnification images (1000x) then serve as an input parameter for the determination of the effective acoustic contrast factor Φ eff , based on a single-cell tracking. In the case of bacteria, experimental videos are segmented to aid the tracking; many individual bacteria are tracked across several experiments, yielding an average Φ eff of a sample. White scale bar corresponds to 10 µm, black scale bar corresponds to 100 µm.

figure.head: Fig. 2
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2 Acoustic focusing of Legionella species, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa in a standing acoustic wave. (a) & (b) Time evolution of line widths of all the investigated species, from two series of focusing experiments; pa = 210 ± 8.4 kPa in series 1 and pa = 190 ± 13.7 kPa in series 2, while the frequency was constant across the two series at f = 3829 kHz. (c) Time evolution of line widths of L. pneumophila subjected to different conditioning protocols; pa = 352 ± 19.7 kPa and f = 3941 kHz. (d) Snapshots of segmented binary images of L. pneumophila and L. fraseri at t = 0 s and t = 20 s, and comparison of the line widths (shaded areas) between the two species, defined as two standard deviations σ (y i ) of the y-position of all the bacteria in the observed frame of 292 µm × 164 µm (the snapshots are half of the actually analyzed length in the x-direction). The insets show a large difference in the morphology of the two subspecies; white scale bar corresponds to 5 µm, black scale bar corresponds to 40 µm. The line width curves in (a)-(c) represent a mean of 2σ (y i ) from 4-8 repetitions of experiments; shaded area surrounding the curves is a standard deviation of the mean 2σ (y i ) across the repetitions. The vertical dashed line in (a) represents a reference timestamp at t = 20 s, while the horizontal dashed line corresponds to a reference line width of 2σ (y i ) = 25 µm.

figure.head: Fig. 3
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3 Experimentally observed variation in shape and size between investigated bacterial species. (a) Legionella spp. investigated in experimental series 1 and 2; (b) E. coli and (c) P. aeruginosa investigated in series 2; (d) conditioned L. pneumophila investigated in series 3. Images are obtained by a high-magnification (1000x) phase-contrast microscopy. Red spheres next to L. longbeachae are reference polystyrene particles with the diameter of 2.21 µm. Scale bar corresponds to 5 µm.

2(a) and 2(b), corresponding to two separate experimental sessions, at f = 3829 kHz and with the average pressure amplitudes of 210 ± 8.4 kPa in series 1 and 190 ± 13.7 kPa in series 2. In the experiments of series 3 (f = 3941 kHz, pa = 352 ± 19.7 kPa) in Fig. 2(c), the acoustic focusing is analyzed for samples of L. pneumophila that were subjected to different conditioning protocols.

figure.head: ø
figure.figDesc: (a)-(b) were used to measure the effective acoustic contrast factor Φ eff of the bacterial species, shown in Fig.4(a). The mean measured Φ eff varies between 0.28 ± 0.089 for L. anisa and 0.076 ± 0.029 for L. parisiensis. In terms of the rate of focusing from Fig.2, L. parisiensis focuses significantly faster than L. micdadei (Φ eff = 0.23 ± 0.069), despite the greater Φ eff of the latter and the lower pa in experimental series 2. The faster focusing of L. parisiensis can be attributed to the larger volume (ESR = 0.86±0.29 µm) compared to L. micdadei (ESR = 0.44±0.097 µm), since v v v obj ∝ Φ eff ESR 2 . P. aeruginosa and L. dumoffii were ex-c (cells/mL)

figure.head: 5 N = 5 N = 5 N = 8 Fig. 4 Fig. 5
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4 Experimentally determined effective acoustic contrast factor Φ eff of the bacterial species. (a) Mean Φ eff for all the bacterial species, averaged over N = 285 -1290 cell trajectories, obtained from 4-8 experiments per species. (b) The corresponding equivalent sphere radius (ESR) measured from high-magnification images (1000x) for N = 19 -154 cells per species. (c) The corresponding initial cell concentration φc. (d) Mean Φ eff for L. pneumophila conditioned in BYE and tap water, for 7 days and for 2 months. (e) The corresponding ESR measured from high-magnification images (1000x) for N = 28 -197 cells per sample. (f) The corresponding φc. Errorbars in the figure represent the standard deviation from the mean value.

figure.head: Fig. 6
figure.figDesc: Fig.6 Influence of collective hydrodynamic focusing (CHF) in computations and experiments. (a) Extracted ratio between the multi-body Φ eff and an assigned single-body Φ from the multi-body numerical simulations in dependence of the initial cell concentration φc, for the experimental particle size distribution of L. pneumophila. (b) Influence of individual contributions to the computational model on the extracted Φ eff for Φ = 0.1 (dashed line) and φc = 4 • 10 8 cells/mL (reference -the mean Φ eff is given by the dash-dotted line): assigning the mean ESR of L. pneumophila to all the particles in the simulation (uniform size); neglecting the Brownian motion (w/o Brownian); neglecting the near-field lubrication interactions (w/o lubrication); neglecting the many-body hydrodynamic interactions (w/o M ∞ ); using a more accurate FTS algorithm24 of Stokesian dynamics that also accounts for stresslets (FTS); increasing the temperature that increases the Brownian motion (T = 350 K); including the inviscid acoustic interactions27 (with AIF). (c) Experimental estimation of the influence of φc on the measured Φ eff for a series of experiments with L. pneumophila in a 200 µm wide channel, performed at f = 3930.9 kHz, with pa = 382 ± 30.1 kPa and ESR = 0.69 ± 0.17 µm.

Bacterial strains used in the study: Legionella pneumophila Philadelphia-1 (DSM 7513), Legionella pneumophila subsp. fraseri (DSM 7514), Legionella pneumophila subsp. pascullei (DSM 7515), Legionella anisa (DSM 17627), Legionella cherrii (DSM 19213), Legionella micdadei (DSM 16640), Legionella dumoffii (DSM 17625), Legionella parisiensis (DSM 19216), Legionella israelensis (DSM 19235), Legionella gormanii (DSM 16641), Legionella longbeachae (DSM 10572), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PAO1), and Escherichia coli (ATCC25922).

figure.head: Figure S1 :Figure S2 :
figure.label: S1S2
figure.figDesc: Figure S1: Experimentally observed variation in shape and size between investigated A. castellanii co-cultured with L. pneumophila from Fig. 5 of the main manuscript. Images are obtained by a highmagnification (1000x) phase-contrast microscopy. Red and white spheres next to amoebae are reference polystyrene particles with the diameter of 2.21 µm and 4.97 µm, respectively. Scale bar corresponds to 10 µm.

Figure S3: Comparison of the measured Φ eff of L. pneumophila across individual experiments. Red line indicates the median; upper and bottom lines of each box (blue lines), indicate the 75th and 25th percentile, respectively; whiskers indicate the maximum and the minimum; and the red pluses represent the outliers. In Fig. 4 of the main manuscript, all the measurement points from individual experiments of a certain species or sample are combined into a single population.

figure.head: Figure S4 :
figure.label: S4
figure.figDesc: Figure S4: Locally measured Φ eff (dots) of L. pneumophila through eq. (8) of the main manuscript, using a local average velocity v obj • e y computed as the path along the y-direction in a time interval of ∆t = 0.5 s, divided by ∆t. The locally measured Φ eff is represented through a moving average with a window of ±5 µm (dashed line). Overall average of the locally measured Φ eff (solid line), and the average Φ eff (dotted line) computed through fitting the theoretical trajectory to experimental trajectories indicate minimal difference, which can be interpreted as both ways of measuring Φ eff giving a comparable average, despite local variations of Φ eff along y.

figure.head: Figure S5 :
figure.label: S5
figure.figDesc: Figure S5: Temperature T of the experimental device used for experiments with bacteria (200 µm channel width) measured during operation under conditions corresponding to experimental series 2 (f = 3829 kHz, p a = 190 ± 13.7 kPa). The temperature was measured with a Pt1000 sensor that was connected to the device with a thermal paste, as indicated in Fig. 1(a) of the main manuscript.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: We would like to acknowledge funding from InnoSuisse through the contract number 49528.1 for the project Contactless separation and concentration of Legionella species in drinking water installations, and the support of the project partners at Georg Fischer JRG AG: Stephan Buerli, Philippe Cachot, Enrico Camelin, Antonio de Agostini, Steffen Lehmann, Hop Nguyen, and Simon Obrist. We thank Mingzhen Fan and Mathias Horn for providing A. castellanii. We thank Thierry Baasch for supporting the imple-

org.orgName: concentration of Legionella species in drinking water installations

div.head: Data availability
div.p: Data is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

div.head: Author Contributions
div.p: All authors designed the research; A.P. designed and built the experimental devices; M.V. prepared all biological samples; A.P. and M.V. conducted the experiments; A.P. developed the code for post-processing the experimental results; A.P. and M.V. postprocessed all the experimental results; A.P. established the computational code, carried out the simulations, and analyzed the data; A.P. prepared the initial manuscript draft; all authors contributed to scientific discussion; all authors commented on and contributed to the writing of the manuscript.

div.head: Conflicts of interest
div.p: There are no conflicts to declare.

monogr.title: Legionella and the prevention of legionellosis
The Journal of the International Society for Analytical Cytology
Guidelines for drinking-water qual-ity, 4th edition, incorporating the 1st addendum
The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America
IEEE transactions on sonics and ultrasonics
Low Reynolds number hydrodynamics: with special applications to particulate media
Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series A: Mathematical and Physical Sciences
Acta Crystallographica Section D: Biological Crystallography
